title: Designing GPU-Accelerated Query Engines with NVIDIA GQE | NVIDIA Technical Blog
gist: NVIDIA's GQE reference architecture uses GB200/Blackwell hardware features (HBM, NVLink-C2C, a dedicated Decompression Engine) plus hybrid compression and partition pruning to run TPC-H SF1000 (1TB) in 9.0s versus 74.0s for DuckDB, a 7.5x aggregate speedup with per-query gains up to 25.5x — a direct push by NVIDIA into the GPU-accelerated database/analytics market.

insights.claim: NVIDIA's GQE ran all 22 TPC-H SF1000 (1TB) queries in 9.0 seconds on a single B200 GPU versus 74.0s (single-socket) and 70.6s (dual-socket) for DuckDB 1.4.1 on an AMD Turin Epyc 9755 CPU, a 7.5x aggregate speedup.
insights.speaker: Clemens Lutz
insights.so_what: A benchmarked 7.5x GPU-over-CPU analytics advantage signals NVIDIA targeting the data-warehouse/OLAP market as its next major GPU demand vector beyond AI training.
insights.quote: GQE runs all queries in 9.0 s, compared to 74.0 s and 70.6 s for DuckDB in single and dual-socket configurations, respectively.
insights.locator: In Figure 6, we show the runtime of the 22 queries

insights.claim: GQE delivered per-query speedups up to 25.5x over DuckDB's best CPU socket configuration, outperforming on 20 of 22 queries and reaching 3x or higher on 17 of them.
insights.speaker: Clemens Lutz
insights.so_what: Broad, not cherry-picked, wins across a standard benchmark strengthen the case that GPU acceleration is viable for mainstream SQL analytics, threatening CPU-only database vendors.
insights.quote: GQE delivers up to 25.5x over DuckDB’s best CPU socket configuration, outperforming it on 20 of 22 queries and reaching 3x or higher on 17.
insights.locator: We present the speedups in Figure 7

insights.claim: NVIDIA explicitly notes the TPC-H results are not TPC-compliant and not comparable to published TPC-H numbers, and that GQE parameters were tuned per query.
insights.speaker: Clemens Lutz
insights.so_what: Per-query tuning and non-compliance caveats mean real-world, untuned gains may be lower — discount the headline multiples when modeling adoption.
insights.quote: the test results in this blog do not comply with the TPC-H specification
insights.locator: The test results in this blog post are derived from TPC-H

insights.claim: GQE is offered as an open-source reference architecture built on NVIDIA cuDF, CCCL, nvCOMP, and nvSHMEM, positioning NVIDIA to seed the design patterns rather than ship a competing database product directly.
insights.speaker: Clemens Lutz
insights.so_what: By open-sourcing the blueprint, NVIDIA drives database vendors to adopt GPU execution on its hardware without antagonizing them as a direct competitor — an ecosystem land-grab.
insights.quote: Leverage the GQE open-source reference architecture and design, and performance optimizations, and explore how GQE can accelerate your data platforms.
insights.locator: Apply GQE best practices to data platforms

insights.claim: NVIDIA introduced a dedicated Decompression Engine in the Blackwell architecture that decompresses LZ77-based formats (LZ4, Snappy, Deflate) without consuming streaming multiprocessor resources, freeing SMs for compute.
insights.speaker: Clemens Lutz
insights.so_what: Fixed-function decompression is a hardware moat feature that CPU and rival GPU vendors lack, differentiating Blackwell for data-movement-bound analytics.
insights.quote: NVIDIA introduced a new Decompression Engine (DE) in the NVIDIA Blackwell architecture that enables nvCOMP to quickly decompress LZ77-based formats like LZ4, Snappy, and Deflate without using SM resources.

insights.claim: The Blackwell Decompression Engine on a single B200 reaches up to 400 GB/s in database applications, achieving ~400 GB/s effective host-to-device throughput at a 4x compression ratio while leaving ~100 GB/s of C2C bandwidth free for other transfers.
insights.speaker: Clemens Lutz
insights.so_what: Compression effectively multiplies scarce interconnect bandwidth, meaning Blackwell's value in data-heavy workloads exceeds raw FLOPS comparisons.
insights.quote: at a 4x compression ratio, it achieves roughly 400 GB/s effective host-to-device throughput while leaving 100 GB/s C2C host-to-device bandwidth available
insights.locator: DE on a single NVIDIA Blackwell B200 GPU

insights.claim: GPU-accelerated query engines are fundamentally constrained by memory and I/O bandwidth rather than compute, so NVIDIA's HBM, NVLink-C2C, and decompression-engine advances target the actual bottleneck.
insights.speaker: Clemens Lutz
insights.so_what: Frames the analytics GPU value proposition around data movement, favoring NVIDIA's tightly-coupled Grace-Blackwell memory architecture over discrete GPU competitors.
insights.quote: GPU-accelerated query engines are often constrained by memory and I/O bandwidth.
insights.locator: GPU-accelerated query engines are often constrained

insights.claim: The benchmark ran on one of two B200 GPUs in a GB200 NVL4 server with B200s connected to the Grace CPU via NVLink-C2C, showcasing the Grace-Blackwell coherent-memory system as the reference platform for GPU analytics.
insights.speaker: Clemens Lutz
insights.so_what: The tight CPU-GPU C2C link is central to the performance story, reinforcing demand for full Grace-Blackwell systems rather than standalone GPUs.
insights.quote: one of the two B200 GPUs in an NVIDIA GB200 NVL4 server, where B200 GPUs are connected to the Grace CPU with NVLink-C2C
insights.locator: To evaluate the B200 GPU features

insights.claim: GQE uses a hybrid compression strategy via nvCOMP that automatically tries both LZ4 and Cascaded algorithms per column, choosing Cascaded only when it beats LZ4's ratio by a configurable multiple, balancing C2C bandwidth, Decompression Engine, and SM resources.
insights.speaker: Clemens Lutz
insights.so_what: Automatic per-column algorithm selection removes a manual tuning barrier for large databases, a usability lever that accelerates enterprise adoption.
insights.quote: The approach we’ve taken is to attempt both LZ4 and Cascaded.
insights.locator: When considering how to compress a given column

insights.claim: The Cascaded format compresses at approximately 500 GB/s on B200, fast enough that trying it as a second algorithm during data loading adds no significant overhead.
insights.speaker: Clemens Lutz
insights.so_what: Near-free trial compression enables the auto-selection heuristic economically, differentiating GPU-side compression from slower CPU approaches.
insights.quote: Cascaded can achieve extremely fast compression rates, at approximately 500 GB/s on B200.
insights.locator: To determine the compression algorithm to use

insights.claim: GQE introduces a batched transfer optimization moving multiple partitions to the GPU in a single cudaMemcpyBatchAsync call, reducing overhead for fine-grained partitions and avoiding delays from interleaved CUDA streams.
insights.speaker: Clemens Lutz
insights.so_what: Micro-optimizations in transfer orchestration are where GPU databases claw back overhead; indicates the maturity of NVIDIA's CUDA software stack for analytics.
insights.quote: Multiple partitions are transferred to the GPU in a single batch using cudaMemcpyBatchAsync, reducing overhead for fine-grained partitions.
insights.locator: In GQE, we conceive a novel batched transfer optimization

insights.claim: GQE transfers data chunks to the GPU on-demand to saturate it with work without storing the full dataset in GPU memory, using pipeline parallelism across four stages (scheduling, H2D transfer, decompression, compute) so runtime ideally equals only the longest stage.
insights.speaker: Clemens Lutz
insights.so_what: On-demand chunked streaming lets GPUs process datasets far larger than their memory, expanding the addressable dataset size and weakening the 'GPUs have too little memory' objection.
insights.quote: GQE transfers data chunks to the GPU on-demand to saturate the GPU with work without storing the full dataset in GPU memory.
insights.locator: The data layer stores and organizes user data

insights.claim: GQE's zone-map-based filter pruning skipped 31% of data across all 22 TPC-H queries on the 1TB dataset, delivering a 1.43x end-to-end speedup on its own.
insights.speaker: Clemens Lutz
insights.so_what: Skipping data before it ever crosses the interconnect is the highest-leverage optimization for bandwidth-bound GPU analytics; quantifies a repeatable design pattern.
insights.quote: filter pruning skips 31% of data across all 22 queries. The impact is an end-to-end speedup of 1.43×.
insights.locator: Filter pruning in GQE is highly effective

insights.claim: GQE computes min/max zone maps per fixed-size partition (default 10M rows) stored as cuDF tables in GPU memory, adding only ~1% to initial Parquet load time and ~2.2 ms average pruning overhead per query, so pruning never becomes a runtime bottleneck.
insights.speaker: Clemens Lutz
insights.so_what: Near-zero-cost metadata that yields double-digit data-skip rates is a favorable cost/benefit that database vendors can readily replicate on GPU.
insights.quote: Computing the zone maps adds about 1% to the initial Parquet load time and happens only once, not during query execution.

insights.claim: The largest GQE wins came on TPC-H Q11, Q14, and Q15 where partition pruning and compression sharply cut data movement across NVLink-C2C, while even bandwidth-heavy queries Q1 and Q6 still executed quickly.
insights.speaker: Clemens Lutz
insights.so_what: Confirms data-movement reduction, not raw compute, is the dominant source of GPU database advantage — guides where the architecture wins vs CPU.
insights.quote: with the largest gains on Q11, Q14, and Q15, where partition pruning and compression sharply cut data movement across NVLink C2C
insights.locator: In Figure 6, we show the runtime of the 22 queries

insights.claim: GQE's query layer natively accepts Substrait plans, letting a database export query plans from an existing product and run them in GQE to evaluate GPU execution benefits, with Apache DataFusion shown transforming SQL into Substrait.
insights.speaker: Clemens Lutz
insights.so_what: Standards-based plan ingestion lowers integration friction for incumbent databases to test GPU offload, a deliberate on-ramp to NVIDIA hardware.
insights.quote: Substrait makes it possible to evaluate the benefits of GPU execution by exporting query plans from an existing database product and running the plan in GQE.
insights.locator: The query layer complements the execution engine
